inputs.label: standard
inputs.project: BLACKOUT — a podcast about power grid failures, energy infrastructure, and the politics of electricity
inputs.tone: Technical but human. The feeling of a well-reported piece in Wired or Bloomberg Businessweek.
inputs.color: Industrial — steel grey, warning amber, electrical blue accent
inputs.audience: Professionals in energy, policy, and journalism aged 30-55
inputs.references: The visual language of Bloomberg Businessweek features, the photography of Edward Burtynsky, industrial infrastructure
inputs.avoid: Stock photo power lines against sunset, green-energy marketing imagery

inputs.label: european
inputs.project: BORDER STORIES — a documentary series about life in European border towns, from the Finnish-Russian border to Gibraltar to the Polish-Ukrainian frontier
inputs.tone: Observational, patient, respectful of the specific. Not dramatic — the everyday reality of people who live where countries meet.
inputs.color: Muted, desaturated, season-specific — grey winter light in the north, dusty warm light in the south
inputs.audience: Documentary and journalism audiences in Europe aged 25-50
inputs.references: The photography of Josef Koudelka (specifically Exiles), the cinema of Aki Kaurismäki, the visual register of ARTE documentaries
inputs.avoid: Political map graphics, barbed-wire clichés, passport/checkpoint stock imagery

inputs.label: arabic-calligraphy-modern
inputs.project: SCRIPT — a digital magazine about Arabic typography, calligraphy, and the intersection of traditional Islamic art with modern graphic design
inputs.tone: Reverent toward tradition, excited about innovation. The feeling of a boutique design studio that respects its roots.
inputs.color: Rich, warm — deep indigo, burnished gold, ivory, touches of terracotta. Not sterile minimalism.
inputs.audience: Arabic-speaking designers, typographers, and art directors aged 25-45
inputs.references: The work of Wissam Shawkat, the geometric patterns of the Alhambra, contemporary Middle Eastern design magazines like Brownbook (RIP) and Kalimat
inputs.avoid: Orientalist clichés, mosque-dome-and-minaret stock images, treating Arabic script as decorative exotica rather than living typography

inputs.label: contradictory-brief
inputs.project: A true crime podcast that is also somehow cozy and comforting — listeners say it helps them fall asleep
inputs.tone: This is the contradiction: it covers murder cases but the audience experiences it as calming background listening. The visual identity must thread that needle without being tasteless.
inputs.color: Dark but not aggressive — deep navy, soft black, muted mauve. Warm, not cold.
inputs.audience: Women 25-45 who listen in bed before sleep
inputs.references: The visual warmth of a dimly lit living room, not the clinical aesthetic of a crime scene investigation
inputs.avoid: Crime scene tape, blood spatter, mugshots, anything that looks like a cable TV true crime show

inputs.label: accessibility-first
inputs.project: SEEN — a documentary photography exhibition about disability and daily life, touring 6 European cities
inputs.tone: Dignified, warm, never pitying. The subjects are people, not cases. The exhibition celebrates ordinary moments, not overcoming narratives.
inputs.color: High contrast for accessibility — must work for visitors with low vision. Colour palette should be distinguishable to common forms of colour blindness.
inputs.audience: General public, disability communities, photography enthusiasts, gallery-goers aged 20-70
inputs.references: The portraiture of Nan Goldin, the design accessibility of the Wellcome Collection, the Centre Pompidou's clear wayfinding system
inputs.avoid: Wheelchairs as visual shorthand for all disability, inspiration porn, any imagery that centres the viewer's emotion over the subject's agency
